Double barrel low pressure systems brought a winter storm to Delaware on the 1st. A combination of snow, sleet and especially freezing rain affected the state mainly during the second half of the day on the 1st. Snowfall amounts were around an inch, but around one quarter of an inch of ice accumulated on exposed surfaces and caused scattered power outages. In addition, the icy precipitation combination caused numerous accidents.||Precipitation started as snow between 9 a.m. and 10 a.m. EST on the 1st throughout most of Delaware. Precipitation changed briefly to sleet in Kent and Sussex Counties and for a more protracted period in New Castle County starting  during the late morning on the 1st. Precipitation changed to all freezing rain in Sussex County around Noon EST, Kent County during the early afternoon and New Castle County by mid afternoon on the 1st. Precipitation changed to plain rain in Sussex County during the early evening on the 1st, but remained freezing rain in the rest of the state until it ended in the middle of the evening on the 1st. ||Delaware State police responded to about 130 traffic accidents, 13 with injuries and around 25 disabled vehicles. Delmarva Power reported about 3,900 of its customers lost power because of downed tree limbs and wires in Delaware and Eastern Maryland. The greatest concentration in Delaware was around Felton in Kent County. Many schools had delayed openings on Monday March 2nd, some schools cancelled classes.| |Representative ice accumulation included 0.33 inches in Pike Creek (New Castle County), 0.25 inches in Camden (Kent County), Frankford (Sussex County) and Newark (New Castle County) and 0.20 inches at the New Castle County Airport.||Representative snowfall (and sleet) included 1.0 inch in Greenville (New Castle County), 0.8 inches in Frederica (Kent County), 0.6 inches in Felton (Kent County), 0.5 inches in Bridgeville and Seaford (Sussex County) and 0.3 inches at the New Castle County Airport.||The winter storm was caused by the combination of an arctic high pressure system that left plenty of cold air in place near the surface and a low pressure system from the South that pumped precipitation into the region.  At 7 a.m. EST on the 1st, a pair of low pressure systems were in Mississippi and the western Gulf of Mexico off the Texas coast.  During the early afternoon, while the low pressure system moved into the Tennessee Valley,  a new low pressure system was forming off of the South Carolina coast.  The latter low pressure system moved rapidly northeast and was off the New Jersey coast at 7 p.m. EST on the 1st while the latter low pressure system became part of a series of lows along a cold front west of the Appalachians. The coastal low pressure system passed near Nantucket, Massachusetts around Midnight EST on the 2nd. It took most of the associated moisture and precipitation with it as the inland low pressure systems and frontal boundary weakened overnight.

An area of moderate to heavy rain with isolated thunderstorms moved through Delaware during the first half of the day on the 20th. One to two inches of rain fell throughout the state with the most widespread heavier amounts in New Castle County. In New Castle County, this led to poor drainage as well as isolated river flooding.  The rain began around 2 a.m. EDT, fell at its heaviest between 5 a.m. and 8 a.m. EDT and ended by 11 a.m. EDT on the 20th. The heavy rain preceded and accompanied the passage of a warm front through Delaware on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poor drainage flooding was reported along Governor Printz Boulevard in Wilmington. In addition, minor flooding occurred along the Christina River.  The Christina River at Coochs Bridge was above its 10.5 foot flood stage from 908 a.m. EDT through 1143 a.m. EDT on the 20th. It crested at 11.22 feet at 1045 a.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals through the morning of the 20th included 2.20 inches in Talley Brook, 2.11 inches in Greenville, 1.88 inches in Newark and 1.77 inches at the New Castle County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent onshore flow caused periods of heavy rain, strong to high winds, beach erosion, and moderate tidal flooding to occur across Delaware from the 1st through the 4th. Strong winds and minor tidal flooding started with the daytime high tide cycle on the 1st. The winds and minor to moderate tidal flooding both peaked on the 2nd. Slow improvement in conditions occurred through Sunday the 4th, with only some residual minor tidal flooding occurring on the 5th.  ||Delaware Governor Jack Markell declared a limited State of Emergency for Sussex County at the onset of the widespread and significant coastal flooding on Friday the 2nd. In Sussex County, Delaware State Route 1 was closed at the Indian River Inlet Bridge due to coastal flooding, and Pilottown Road in Lewes was also closed due to flooding. In Kent County, a portion of Fork Landing Road west of Frederica was closed due to flooding, and the intersection of Old State Road and Old Corbit Road in Middletown was impassable and closed due to flooding. Numerous road closures were also reported around Big Stone Beach in Kent County due to tidal flooding. Significant beach erosion occurred along Delaware's southern coast, with minor to moderate damage to dunes being reported. Meanwhile in New Castle County, saturated ground and gusty winds downed a large tree along Weatherhill Drive in Weatherhill Farms west of Wilmington.   ||Minor tidal flooding began with the day and night high tide cycles on the 1st in Sussex and Kent Counties, escalating to moderate tidal flooding and the highest tides occurring during the daytime high tide cycle on the 2nd. The high tide at Lewes (Breakwater Harbor) reached 7.57 feet above mean lower low water at 106 p.m. EDT on the 2nd. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.0 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding continued at Lewes (Breakwater Harbor) with the next two successive high tide cycles, the early morning and early afternoon of the 3rd. Minor tidal flooding then occurred at Lewes with successive early morning and afternoon high tide cycles from the 4th through the 5th. In addition, minor tidal flooding also occurred along the lower part of the tidal Delaware River at Reedy Island, DE with late afternoon high tide cycles on the 3rd and the 4th, affecting portions of the New Castle County coast.||The peak in tidal flooding, especially from the afternoon of the 2nd through the early morning of the 3rd, also coincided with the strongest winds to affect the area as most of the peak wind gusts occurred during this timeframe. Measured wind gusts to affect Delaware included 62 mph at the Lewes NOS buoy (Sussex County), 48 mph at the Sussex County Airport (Sussex County), 46 mph at Indian River Bay (Sussex County), 45 mph at Rehoboth Beach (Sussex County), 43 mph at the New Castle County Airport (New Castle County), and 40 mph at Dover Air Force Base (Kent County).  ||The tidal flooding and strong winds were caused by a strengthening pressure gradient (difference) between a strong high pressure system building across southeastern Canada and a lingering frontal boundary offshore of the Northeast and Mid Atlantic seaboard. In addition, some moisture, associated with Hurricane Joaquin passing offshore well to the south, interacted with the stalled frontal boundary, which enhanced rainfall amounts somewhat across the area. Storm total rainfall amounts averaged from 2 to 4.5 inches, especially across Sussex and Kent Counties, with the heaviest amounts being recorded near the coast. The pressure gradient then weakened during the day of the 4th as the high pressure across southeast Canada weakened.

In addition, minor tidal flooding also occurred along the lower part of the tidal Delaware River at Reedy Island, DE with late afternoon high tide cycles on the 3rd and the 4th, affecting portions of the New Castle County coast.||The peak in tidal flooding, especially from the afternoon of the 2nd through the early morning of the 3rd, also coincided with the strongest winds to affect the area as most of the peak wind gusts occurred during this timeframe. Measured wind gusts to affect Delaware included 62 mph at the Lewes NOS buoy (Sussex County), 48 mph at the Sussex County Airport (Sussex County), 46 mph at Indian River Bay (Sussex County), 45 mph at Rehoboth Beach (Sussex County), 43 mph at the New Castle County Airport (New Castle County), and 40 mph at Dover Air Force Base (Kent County).  ||The tidal flooding and strong winds were caused by a strengthening pressure gradient (difference) between a strong high pressure system building across southeastern Canada and a lingering frontal boundary offshore of the Northeast and Mid Atlantic seaboard. In addition, some moisture, associated with Hurricane Joaquin passing offshore well to the south, interacted with the stalled frontal boundary, which enhanced rainfall amounts somewhat across the area. Storm total rainfall amounts averaged from 2 to 4.5 inches, especially across Sussex and Kent Counties, with the heaviest amounts being recorded near the coast. The pressure gradient then weakened during the day of the 4th as the high pressure across southeast Canada weakened.

A persistent onshore flow caused periods of heavy rain, strong to high winds, beach erosion, and moderate tidal flooding to occur across Delaware from the 1st through the 4th. Strong winds and minor tidal flooding started with the daytime high tide cycle on the 1st. The winds and minor to moderate tidal flooding both peaked on the 2nd. Slow improvement in conditions occurred through Sunday the 4th, with only some residual minor tidal flooding occurring on the 5th.  ||Delaware Governor Jack Markell declared a limited State of Emergency for Sussex County at the onset of the widespread and significant coastal flooding on Friday the 2nd. In Sussex County, Delaware State Route 1 was closed at the Indian River Inlet Bridge due to coastal flooding, and Pilottown Road in Lewes was also closed due to flooding. In Kent County, a portion of Fork Landing Road west of Frederica was closed due to flooding, and the intersection of Old State Road and Old Corbit Road in Middletown was impassable and closed due to flooding. Numerous road closures were also reported around Big Stone Beach in Kent County due to tidal flooding. Significant beach erosion occurred along Delaware's southern coast, with minor to moderate damage to dunes being reported. Meanwhile in New Castle County, saturated ground and gusty winds downed a large tree along Weatherhill Drive in Weatherhill Farms west of Wilmington.   ||Minor tidal flooding began with the day and night high tide cycles on the 1st in Sussex and Kent Counties, escalating to moderate tidal flooding and the highest tides occurring during the daytime high tide cycle on the 2nd. The persistent northeast flow due to departing low pressure off the northern Mid Atlantic coast and high pressure over the Eastern Seaboard produced a higher, generally moderate risk of dangerous rip currents on the 9th along Atlantic coastal Delaware. There were 54 rescues at Bethany Beach on Sunday the 9th, with a total of 72 rescues through the entire weekend. In addition, over 100 rescues were reported at Rehoboth Beach on the 9th.

A continued northeast flow around departing low pressure from the North Carolina coast produced a higher, generally moderate risk of stronger rip currents on the 8th along Atlantic coastal Delaware. There were 15 rescues at Dewey Beach (Sussex County). Feedback from lifeguards indicated that those entering the water were restricted to either knee or waist deep, and as a result, this potentially cut back on the number of rescues.

A high pressure system centered over the eastern Great Lakes produced an onshore, northeast flow throughout the day on the 22nd that enhanced the strength of the rip currents along the Delaware coast. At Bethany Beach, 39 people were rescued from rip currents.

A line of strong to severe thunderstorms formed in the warm sector of the Maryland panhandle moved through Delaware during the evening on the 9th. The line also spawned an EF-0 tornado in New Castle County.  About 3,700 homes and businesses lost power in New Castle County.  All power was restored by the evening of the 10th. A warm front lifted northeast into central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 9th. This permitted sufficient daytime heating preceding an approaching cold front. In addition, there was enough shear in the atmosphere for a tornado to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the tornado wind damage in the area, the parent severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and wires along Delaware State Route 9 between Lambson Lane and Terminal Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough preceding an approaching cold front helped trigger a cluster of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain over southern Kent as well as eastern Sussex County in Delaware during the afternoon of the 21st. One thunderstorm caused flash flooding around the Rehoboth Beach area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller creeks as well as roadway flash flooding in Lewes and Rehoboth Hundred. Both Old Landing and Airport Roads were flooded and closed. Event precipitation totals included 2.51 inches in Lewes, 2.16 inches in Rehoboth Beach, 2.01 inches in Nassau and 1.67 inches in Milton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalling frontal boundary triggered widely scattered thunderstorms with very heavy rain in Delaware. One thunderstorm interacted with a sea breeze front and remained nearly stationary over southeast Sussex County and caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary thunderstorm caused flash flooding of smaller creeks and roadways in extreme southeast Sussex County in Baltimore Hundred.   Doppler Radar storm total estimates approached four inches along the Delaware State Route 54 corridor approaching Fenwick Island.  A CoCoRaHS observer east of Selbyville measured 2.60 inches of rain which all fell within two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unseasonably hot and humid air mass, an approaching cold front and strong winds aloft caused a squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms to move through Delaware during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd. Hardest hit was the northern part of the state. A wind gust of 66 mph was recorded at the New Castle County Airport.  Delmarva Power and Light reported 63,000 of its customers lost power on the Delmarva Peninsula (14,000 in New Castle County) and it took until the 26th to fully restore power in Delaware. Verizon phone service was also interrupted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Greenville (Christina Hundred).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms formed in the warm sector of the Maryland panhandle moved through Delaware during the evening on the 9th. The line also spawned an EF-0 tornado in New Castle County.  About 3,700 homes and businesses lost power in New Castle County.  All power was restored by the evening of the 10th. A warm front lifted northeast into central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 9th. This permitted sufficient daytime heating preceding an approaching cold front. In addition, there was enough shear in the atmosphere for a tornado to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado occurred in eastern New Castle County in Minquadale (Brandywine Hundred).  | |The tornado touched down near a trailer park off of New Castle Avenue.  Eight units of the mobile home village and apartments on New Castle Avenue were damaged. A downed transformer went through one bedroom. Shingles and gutters were ripped from other homes. Six families were displaced and the Red Cross opened an emergency shelter at William Penn High School. The tornado damage extended eastward with a telephone pole sheered off and a tree down on Revis Avenue. Farther east, additional damage occurred on Harbor View Drive where roof damage and a wall collapse occurred at an industrial park.  Several trees were also knocked down on Dock View Drive. ||The EF-0 tornado path length was about half a mile with a maximum path width of 75 yards. The estimated maximum wind speeds were 65 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and ensuing fire damaged a home in Milford. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather affected Delaware on the 19th and 20th. High temperatures both days reached into the lower to mid 90s. The 19th was hotter and more humid overall with afternoon heat index values of around 105F. These were some of the highest heat index values of the entire summer. A dissipating cold front on the 20th brought slightly drier air into the state during the afternoon of the 20th and heat index values peaked around 100F. A re-enforcing cold frontal passage on the 21st brought even cooler and drier air into the area and by the 22nd all high temperatures were less than 90 degrees in Delaware. The heat not only affected people. The Brandywine Zoo implemented procedures to keep its animals cool. ||Highest temperatures included 97 degrees in Seaford (Sussex County), 95 degrees in Glasgow (New Castle County), 94 degrees in Georgetown (Sussex County) and Dover (Kent County), 93 degrees in Rehoboth Beach (Sussex County) and 92 degrees at the New Castle County Airport.

The southern edge of a slow moving cold front, extending from southern New Jersey into Delaware and Northeast Maryland, triggered some severe thunderstorms into the central Delmarva Peninsula during the late evening of the 4th where damaging wind gusts and power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Laurel.

A continued southeast flow around a surface high pressure system combined with spring tides associated with the new moon to continue a higher, generally moderate risk of stronger rip currents on the 17th along Atlantic coastal Delaware. There were 46 water rescues at Bethany Beach (Sussex County).

An area of low pressure along the Middle Atlantic coast produced several strong easterly wind gusts during the morning of the 10th in Delaware. Measured wind gusts included a 45 mph gust in Brandywine (New Castle County), and a 43 mph gust was recorded in Lewes (Sussex County) and also at Dewey Beach (Sussex County). The low pressure slowly tracked along the Eastern Seaboard from around the Tidewater of Virginia early in the day, crossing the lower Delmarva Peninsula and eventually emerging off the southern New Jersey coast by the evening. In addition to some strong wind gusts, this coastal area of low pressure also produced rainfall amounts ranging mostly between 1.5 inches to 3.0 inches of rain in Delaware.
